coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of high fluorescence graphene quantum dots and their selective detection for Fe3+ in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ting-Ting
Yang, Jia-Xiang
Song, Ji-Ming
Chen, Jing-Shuai
Niu, He-Lin
Mao, Chang-Jie
Zhang, Sheng-Yi
Shen, Yu-Hua
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon particles have gained numerous attentions because of their simple synthetic process, low toxicity and water solubility. The present work involves synthesis of two types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by treating sulfydryl group (−SH) amino acid glutathione (GSH) in the presence and absence of AgNO3 via one pot route, respectively. The Ag+ ions from AgNO3 can combine the −SH from GSH to form Ag2S, thus surfaces of the obtained graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are mainly modified by some free hydroxyl groups (OH), amino groups (NH2) and carboxyl groups (COOH). In the absence of Ag+ ions, the surfaces of as-prepared graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) not only include OH, NH2 and COOH, but also −SH. Interestingly, as-prepared two kinds of GQDs both have strong fluorescence with different emission wavelength. More importantly, these GQDs have special recognition ability for metal ions. The N-GQDs respond rapidly towards the presence of Fe3+ ions, and can be used as a probe to detect Fe3+ ions in waste water, while the SN-GQDs have a special recognition to Hg2+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Glutathione
Silver nitrate
Fluorescence detection
Iron ion
Mercury ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in real sample
Absorption and emission spectra of sample
Selectivity and specificity of N-GQDs and SN-GQDs
Effect of acidity and alkalinity on the fluorescence property
Standard curve for the determination of Fe3+ ions
Detection of N-CQDs as a sensor to Fe3+ in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful for the support rendered by the National Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No. 21641007, 21471001), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (No. 1508085MB22), Major Project of Education Department of Anhui Province (No. KJ2016SD63) and the Innovation Experimental Fund of Anhui University (No. yqh100041). We also thank The Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Materials Chemistry of Anhui Province for the financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process for N-GQDs and SN-GQDs.

(a) XRD patterns of the N-GQDs; (b) AFM pattern of the N-GQDs; (c,d) TEM images of N-GQDs, inset in (c) shows HRTEM image of an individual nanoparticle, inset in (d) shows size distribution of the N-GQDs.
(a, b) are IR spectrum and Raman spectrum of N-GQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs, (b–d) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively.

(a, b) the fluorescence excitation, emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of samples: (a) N-GQDs, (b) SN-GQDs; Inset in (a) and (b) shows the photographs under visible light (left) and UV light of 365nm (right), respectively; (c) emission spectra of N-GQDs obtained by changing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 370nm; (d) emission spectra of SN-GQDs obtained by changing the excitation wavelength from 347 to 420nm.

(a) The fluorescence response of N-GQDs (Ex=330nm, [Mn+]=2.0*10−3 M, [N-GQDs]=10mg/mL); (b) The corresponding relative fluorescence intensities before (black) and after (red) the Fe3+ (2.0*10−3 M) was added to the N-GQDs solution in the same condition; (c) The fluorescence response of SN-GQDs (Ex=347nm; [Mn+]=2.0*10−3 M, [SN-GQDs]=10mg/mL); (d) The corresponding relative fluorescence intensities before (black) and after (red) the Hg2+ (2.0*10−3 M) was added to the SN-GQDs solution in the same condition. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The mechanism of selectivity and specificity of (a) N-GQDs and (b) SN-GQDs.

(a)The fluorescence intensity at 425nm of N-GQDs solutions at different pH value; (b) the fluorescence intensity at 425nm of sample after the same amount of Fe3+ ions were added to the N-GQDs solution with different pH ([Fe3+]=2.0*10−3 M, [N-GQDs]=10mg/mL), respectively.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs (10.0g/mL) by treating with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, inset shows the fluorescence quenching photographs of N-GQDs with different Fe3+ ions concentrations (from left to right: 0, 5.0*10−4 M, 1.0*10−3 M, 1.5*10−3 M and 2.0*10−3 M); (b) The relationship between the F/F0 and the Fe3+ ions concentrations.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs (10.0g/mL) by treating with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (from top to bottom: 0, 5.0*10−5 M, 2.0*10−4 M, 5.0*10−4 M, 1.0*10−3 M and 2.5*10−3 M) in lake water. (b) The relationship between the F/F0 and the Fe3+ ions concentrations.
Comparison of the fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ ions between this work and the reported literatures.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of high fluorescence graphene quantum dots and their selective detection for Fe3+ in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Environment Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui, 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Environment Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with different fluorescence emission wavelength were obtained by treating sulfydryl group (-SH) amino acid glutathione (GSH) in the presence and absence of AgNO3 via one pot route, respectively. These GQDs have special recognition ability for metal ions. The one respond rapidly towards the presence of Fe3+ ions, and can be used as a probe to detect Fe3+ ions in waste water, while the other have a special recognition to Hg2+ ions.

In recent years, carbon particles have gained numerous attentions because of their simple synthetic process, low toxicity and water solubility. The present work involves synthesis of two types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by treating sulfydryl group (−SH) amino acid glutathione (GSH) in the presence and absence of AgNO3 via one pot route, respectively. The Ag+ ions from AgNO3 can combine the −SH from GSH to form Ag2S, thus surfaces of the obtained graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are mainly modified by some free hydroxyl groups (OH), amino groups (NH2) and carboxyl groups (COOH). In the absence of Ag+ ions, the surfaces of as-prepared graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) not only include OH, NH2 and COOH, but also −SH. Interestingly, as-prepared two kinds of GQDs both have strong fluorescence with different emission wavelength. More importantly, these GQDs have special recognition ability for metal ions. The N-GQDs respond rapidly towards the presence of Fe3+ ions, and can be used as a probe to detect Fe3+ ions in waste water, while the SN-GQDs have a special recognition to Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, chemosensors have drawn tremendous attention of the scientific community because of their wide applications in various fields, such as environment [1], biological detection [2], and chemical industry [3]. There are many diverse types of chemosensors, such as, gas sensor [4], humidity sensor [5], biosensor [6] and ion sensor [7,8]. In the present century, fluorescent chemosensors are widely used to detect ions in solution, owing to their advantage of high sensitivity, simplicity and selectivity [9,10]. These advantages lead to the synthesis of many new fluorescent chemosensors. For instance, Gupta and his co-workers prepared two novel fluorescent rhodamine derivatives with a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent and colorimetric response to Cr3+ ions [11]. Batista et al. have synthesized a series of arylfuryl-bis(unduly)methane derivatives, which have a high selective recognition of Hg2+ ions in organic aqueous mixture [12].

Iron (Fe) is one of the most important trace elements in living organisms in order to enable oxygen-carrying capacity [13–15]. There approximately exists 4–5g Fe in human body in different forms. Although Fe plays a vital role in the biological system, excess Fe will cause cell oxidation and annihilation of blood circulation in human body [16]. According to a recent study, Fe is another factor in the primary productivity of phytoplankton besides nitrate, phosphate and silicate [17]. Hence it becomes necessary to develop methods that allow rapid and simple detection of Fe element in solution. So far, there have some of the available approaches for Fe element determination, such as chemiluminescence [18], atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [19], colorimetric methods [20], spectrophotometry [21]. Among these, fluorescence spectrometry has attracted enormous attention, owing to its advantages of high sensitivity and rapid response [22,23].

In this contribution, we successfully synthesized two kinds of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a simple synthetic strategy by hydrothermal treatment of glutathione (GSH). The synthesis process is displayed in Scheme 1 . The results showed that N-GQDs were obtained in the presence of Ag+ ions, while SN-GQDs, obtained in the absence of Ag+ ions when other synthesis conditions were remained unchanged. Interestingly, both GQDs have good water solubility in spite of having different fluorescence. The fluorescence color of SN-GQDs is blue while the fluorescence color of N-GQDs, bluish green. Two kinds of GQDs have special the recognition and response ability for Fe3+ ions (N-GQDs) and Hg2+ ions (SN-GQDs), respectively. Meanwhile, the as-prepared N-GQDs respond rapidly towards the presence of Fe3+ ions, and can be used as a probe to detect Fe3+ ions in waste water.

Glutathione (GSH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), and all other solvents used were of analytical grade. The analyte solutions of metal ions were prepared by dissolving CaCl2, KCl, MgSO4·7H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Na2SO4, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and HgCl2.

The samples were characterized by different analytic techniques. Powder X-Ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained on a Rigaku D/max-RA X-ray diffractometer with CuKα1 radiation (λ=0.15406nm). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images involving high resolution TEM (HRTEM) observation were carried out on a JEOL-2100 TEM with an acceleration voltage of 200KV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were performed on a Hitachi S4800 microscopy operating at 5.0KV. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were recorded by using Micro Nano AFM-Ш. IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR (NEXUS-870) instrument by using KBr discs in the range of 4000–400cm−1. The Raman spectra were recorded with a Labram-HR spectrometer using the 532nm line of the Ar ion for excitation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by using a VG ESCA scientific theta probe spectrometer in constant analyzer energy mode with a pass energy of 28eV and AlKα (1486.6eV) radiation as the excitation source. UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-3600 PC) was used to record diffuse reflectance spectra of samples. The fluorescence spectra were recorded with PerkinElmer LS-55 Fluorescence spectrometer.

The N-GQDs and SN-GQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal route. (1) Synthesis of N-GQDs: 0.034g (0.2mmol) AgNO3, 0.092g (0.3mmol) GSH were added into a teflon-lined stainless autoclave of 25mL capacity in turn. Subsequently, 20mL ethylene glycol was added into the autoclave. The solution was stirred vigorously for 20min and then sealed and maintained at 200°C for 12h, after that, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The product included upper clear liquid and lower sediment, that is to say, GQDs dispersed in the upper clear liquid (ethylene glycol), and the lower sediment was Ag2S nanocrystals by subsequent characterization. They were collected them respectively. The upper clear liquid was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min, and the sediment was abandoned; then ethylene glycol was wiped off by rotary evaporation at 180°C, and the solid state sample (GQDs) was obtained. The lower sediment of the product was washed by distilled water and ethanol absolute three times in turn. The sample was collected for further characterization. (2) Synthesis of SN-GQDs: the synthesis process was carried out similar to that of N-GQDs but in the absence of AgNO3 in the origin reactants mixture, so the product didn’t include Ag2S nanocrystals.

As described in the previous literature [24], 15μL of GQDs aqueous solution (10mg/mL) was diluted to 3mL by distilled water. Different ions solution of Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ were prepared under identical conditions. Because interaction between metal ions and the GQDs could quickly reach balance, there didn’t need incubate after the metal ion solution was added to the GQDs solution. Then a fluorescence spectrum of the mixture solution was recorded.

In a typical experiment, 15μL of GQDs aqueous solution (10mg/mL) was put in a standard quartz cuvette. Then, the certain volume of Fe3+ ions solution was added into the cuvette. The mixed solution was diluted to 3mL by distilled water at room temperature, followed by recording the fluorescence quenching spectra at the respective excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the N-GQDs as a sensor for Fe3+ in real sample, the lake water were chosen as real sample. As described in the previous literature [25], the lake water, which were obtained from the artificial lake in our university, were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min to remove solid impurities. Then, the water sample was prepared as different concentrations of Fe3+ ions solution. The water sample was added to the sensing system and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The N-GQDs were synthesized via one step method under solvothermal condition. To determine the composition of the product, the samples were analyzed by the XRD. The XRD pattern of the product is displayed in Fig. 1 (a). There is a diffraction peak with the range of 20.30°–24.30°, which corresponds to a graphitic structure [26,27]. And the reason of this broadening of the diffraction peak is the small size of N-GQDs [28]. AFM image was displayed in Fig. 1(b). It can be seen that well-dispersed quantum dots were produced in the process. The height profiles reveal that the typical topographic height difference of the N-GQDs is 1.514nm. The literature has reported that the theoretical thickness of a graphene monolayer is 0.34nm [29], so the AFM results demonstrate that the obtained N-GQDs consist of 1–5 graphene layers. Fig. 1(c) and (d) are TEM images of the synthesized N-GQDs. The TEM image indicates that N-GQDs have small spherical morphology with diameter of ca. 2.5 nanometer. In addition, HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 1(c)) demonstrates that the d-spacing of 0.201nm corresponds to (102) plane of graphitic carbon [30]. The inset in Fig. 1(d) shows that the diameters of N-GQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 2.0–3.0nm with an average size of 2.5nm. The lower sediment of product have also been collected and characterized. The XRD pattern shows that the collected precipitate is Ag2S (JCPDS 14-0072) and TEM image displays that the Ag2S have irregular morphology particles (see Supporting Information (SI), Fig. S1). In order to determine the ratio of the reactant (GSH to AgNO3) and explore the function of AgNO3 in whole reaction system, the related experiments have also been finished (see SI, Fig. S2–S3). Although the ratios of reactants is different, all of the GQDs have no IR vibrational absorption of the −SH at range of 2500–2600cm−1, indicating the −SH group fell out of GSH molecule in the presence of AgNO3. However, in the absence of AgNO3 and kept other experiment conditions same, the obtained sample (SN-GQDs) included −SH group from the IR characterization of the SN-GQDs (see SI, Fig. S5). XRD pattern, AFM image and TEM images showed that SN-GQDs have similar diffraction peak and morphology to that of N-GQDs (see SI, Fig. S4). This result revealed that AgNO3 could induce −SH to drop from GSH molecules during solvothermal process.

Fig. 2 (a) is the IR spectrum of N-GQDs. There is a broad peak at around 3380cm−1 which corresponds to the absorption features of stretching vibrations of NH and OH groups. It illustrates that a mass of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-GQDs impart good hydrophilicity to the products [31]. Moreover, the peaks at 1690cm−1 analogous to the CO bond and 1190cm−1 corresponding to the CO groups confirm the presence of the COOH group. The peak at 2930cm−1 corresponds to the CH bond, and the band at 1420cm−1 to the CN bond [32].

Raman spectrum was performed to support the composition of the product. As clearly shown in Fig. 2(b), there are two superimposed broad peaks of D-band and G-band at around 1600 and 1400cm−1, respectively, which were attributed to in-plane vibrations of crystalline graphite and disordered amorphous carbon [33,34]. G-band is higher than D-band, which indicates that the crystalline graphite is more in composition than amorphous carbon. These results are also consistent with the XRD and HRTEM analysis. The IR spectrum and Raman spectrum of SN-GQDs were tested too (see SI, Fig. S5).

XPS survey spectrum of the synthesized N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3 (a), which displays the peaks of S 2p (163.8eV),C 1s (285.4eV),N 1s (399.0eV) and O 1s (531.7eV) [35,36]. To obtain more information about the samples, we performed high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. For the high-resolution C 1s region (Fig. 3(b)), there are three peaks observed at 284.8, 286.0, and 287.5eV, which are attributed to the CC, CO and CO groups, respectively. In Fig. 3(c), we can seen that the binding energy of the N(C)3 and CNC characteristic absorption peak occur at 399.2eV and 400.3eV [37], respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) shows two peaks at 531.3 and 532.3eV, which are assigned to the CO and COH/COC groups [38], respectively. The XPS spectra of SN-GQDs have been tested too (see SI, Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and emission spectra of sample
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectrum of N-GQDs in water solution is shown in Fig. 4 (a). There is a small absorption peak located at 320nm in the UV–vis spectrum. The maximum absorption wavelength of SN-GQDs in water emerged at about 300nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 4(b)). The fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of N-GQDs and SN-GQDs are shown in Fig. 4(a) and Fig. 4(b), respectively. When N-GQDs are excited by the UV light at 330nm, a maximum fluorescence peak of N-GQDs is located at 425nm and a shoulder peak at about 475nm (Fig. 4(a)). The inset in Fig. 4(a) exhibits the color change of N-GQDs solution from pale yellow to bluish green under excitation by UV light at 365nm. When SN-GQDs are excited by the UV light at 347nm, a maximum fluorescence peak of SN-GQDs is located at 430nm (Fig. 4(b)). The inset in Fig. 4(b) exhibits the color change of SN-GQDs solution from pale yellow to blue under excitation by UV light at 365nm. The emission range of N-GQDs was integrated between 300nm and 370nm and the wavelength of maximum emission was always located at about 425nm (Fig. 4(c)). From the above results, it is revealed that the fluorescence emission of N-GQDs is not excitation-dependent. Fig. 4(d) demonstrates that the fluorescence emission of SN-GQDs is excitation-dependent. The fluorescence peak has a clear red shift and fluorescence intensity decreases with the change of excitation wavelength. This phenomenon may due to the distribution of different emissive trap sites, the difference in particle size and multi fluorescence components in SN-GQDs [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and specificity of N-GQDs and SN-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to estimate the special recognition ability of N-GQDs and SN-GQDs, different ions solution of Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, Ba2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+ and Hg2+ were prepared under the identical conditions. All of these prepared metal ions were added in the N-GQDs and SN-GQDs under the same conditions for testing fluorescence response. Fig. 5 (a) and (c) show the fluorescence response of N-GQDs and SN-GQDs. The relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) was used to test whether the quenching behavior of N-GQDs and SN-GQDs by specific ion was selective. The F and F0 in Fig. 5(a) are the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs at 425nm in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively. While for Fig. 5(c), The F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of SN-GQDs at 430nm in the presence and absence of metal ions. Fig. 5(a) and (c) intuitively shows that there are no obvious variation after the addition of Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Na+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and Ni2+. However, when the equal amounts of Fe3+ were added to the N-GQDs solution, the value of F0/F have a obviously enhanced because the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs have a significant drop-off (Fig. 5(a)), while that of SN-GQDs have same phenomenon in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 5(c)). This results shows that N-GQDs have a good recognition to Fe3+ ions, while SN-GQDs to Hg2+ ions. To explore the competition of the ions, we added Fe3+ ions into other metal ions solution with same concentration to inspect the change of relative fluorescence intensities. In Fig. 5(b) and (d), black vertical bar indicates original fluorescent response of metal ions, while red vertical bar, fluorescent response after Fe3+ ions (or Hg2+ ions) being added. We could find that the relative fluorescent intensity were steeply increased by further treatment with equivalent Fe3+ ions (Fig. 5(b)) and Hg2+ ions (Fig. 5(d)) under the same condition.

Selectivity measurements of metal ions showed that N-GQDs had a better recognition to Fe3+ ions than Hg2+ ions whereas SN-GQDs exhibited just the opposite. In addition, both of them displayed no clear response to other metal ions. We thought the above phenomenon could be explained as follows. IR spectra indicated that these two kinds of the GQDs were modified by OH, −NH2 and COOH. The previous literatures have reported that these organic groups have an affinity with Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions [41–43]. Thus, they have more response to Fe3+ ions and Hg2+ ions compared with other ions. Fig. 6 (a) is the diagrammatic representation of action mechanisms of the N-GQDs. Due to the three positive charges of Fe3+ ions, there occurs a strong interaction with these organic groups than Hg2+ ions and other metal ions. Meanwhile, three positive charges had resulted in more electrons transferred to the d orbital of Fe3+ ions [44,45]. Fig. 6(b) is the illustration of action mechanisms of the SN-GQDs. Unlike N-GQDs, there have an excess −SH on the surface of SN-GQDs. As we all know, there is a strong affinity between Hg2+ ions and −SH. The formation of Hg-S bands is a process of effective electron transfer, which facilitates the non-radiative recombination of the excitations [46]. Therefore, N-GQDs have a better response to Fe3+, while SN-GQDs have a better response to Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of acidity and alkalinity on the fluorescence property
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the stability of the fluorescence for N-GQDs, we have tested the fluorescence intensity N-GQDs solution by varying the pH values from 1.5 to 12.5. Fig. 7 (a) shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs aqueous solution has subtle changes at the different pH conditions. After the same amount of Fe3+ ions was added to the above solution, the fluorescence intensity remained essentially stable in the pH range of 3.5–9.5, but a little decreased in pH range of 1.5–2.5 and increased in pH range of 10.5–11.5 (Fig. 7(b)). It demonstrates that fluorescence quenching was weakened at intense alkaline environment, while the quenching was enhanced at intense acidic condition. Because at alkaline environment, Fe3+ ions easily reacted with OH− to generated Fe(OH)3. The results illustrates that the N-GQDs have a better stability under the pH range of 3.5–9.5, and this range can be selected as the optimum pH for the fluorescence quenching.

We also studied the incubation time of the sensing system, as shown in Fig. 8 . Three different concentrations of Fe3+ ions were added to the N-GQDs solution, sequentially, and the fluorescence intensity at 425nm declined rapidly and almost no change was detected after 30s, which indicates that the N-GQDs have a rapid response time for Fe3+ ions. So there is no need for extra incubation time for detection of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curve for the determination of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 (a) displays the fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution with the different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence intensity at 425nm gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ ions concentrations. There is hardly any fluorescence until the Fe3+ ions concentrations reach 2.0*10−3 M. The inset in Fig. 9(a) displays the gradual color change of the solution from bluish green to colorless with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ ions, which is consistent with the fluorescence spectra. And the standard curves for the determination of Fe3+ ions have been displayed in Fig. 9(b). The above result reveals that there is a good linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity, where the F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity at 425nm before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions and the concentrations of Fe3+ ions from 5.0*10−5 to 2.0*10−3 M with a correlation coefficient (R) of −0.9953. The detection limit is estimated to be 70nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N=3). It also demonstrates that N-GQDs can potentially be used as a probe for detecting Fe3+ ions in wastewater.

Compared with current reports (Table 1 ), the prepared N-GQDs have a wider linear range and good linear relation, which demonstrates that it is a promising material for monitoring Fe3+ ions in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of N-CQDs as a sensor to Fe3+ in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the N-GQDs as a sensor to Fe3+ in real sample, the performance of the N-GQDs for real sample analysis was investigated by lake water samples obtained from the artificial lake in our university. Fig. 10 (a) displays that the fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs by treating with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions in lake water. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity at 425nm gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ ions concentrations. The standard curves for determining Fe3+ in the lake water were shown in Fig. 10(b). There is a good linear correlation in the range of 0–2.5*10−3 M with a correlation coefficient (R) of −0.9938. Although there are different kinds of organics and minerals in the lake water, the sensing system have a good detection for Fe3+. It demonstrated that the prepared N-GQDs can be used as a sensor in real environmental samples.

In this work, we have successfully synthesized two kind of water soluble GQDs with different specific recognition ability for metal ions by an effective and facile solvothermal method using GSH as carbon source. Ag+ ions play a key role for synthesis of two kinds of GQDs. In the presence of Ag+ ions, thiol-free N-GQDs were obtained, and this quantum dots had a bright bluish green fluorescence and a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Fe3+ ions within a wide concentration range between 1.0*10−5 and 2.0*10−3 M and the detection limit is estimated to be 70nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N=3). There is no need for incubation period because of quick response. Therefore, the N-GQDs can be potentially used as probe to detect Fe3+ ions in wastewater. However, in the absence of Ag+ ions during synthesis process, SN-GQDs with thiol were prepared, this quantum dots had a bright blue fluorescence, and showed rapid response to the existence of Hg2+ ions. In addition, the present synthetic scheme provides a simple and convenient route for fluorescent quantum dot.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the support rendered by the National Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No. 21641007, 21471001), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (No. 1508085MB22), Major Project of Education Department of Anhui Province (No. KJ2016SD63) and the Innovation Experimental Fund of Anhui University (No. yqh100041). We also thank The Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Materials Chemistry of Anhui Province for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.12.048.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing biologically and environmentally important mental ions with fluorescent chemosensors: thermodynamic versus optical response selectivity in some study case
Coord. Chem. Rev.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ting-ting Xu received her bachelor’s degree in Department of Chemistry, Huainan Normal University in 2014. She is currently pursuing M.S. Degree at Anhui University. Her research interests are the synthesis of fluorescence nanomaterials and their applications in ion sensors.

Jia-xiang Yang received PhD degree in inorganic chemistry from the University of Science and Technology of China in 2001. After three-year postdoctoral research fellowship at Shandong University, he worked at the National University of Singapore as a research fellow in 2008. He is currently a professor in Anhui University. His main research interest is developing new approach of the synthesis of organic optoelectronic materials.

Ji-Ming Song received his Ph.D. degree in inorganic chemistry from University of Science and Technology of China in 2007, and worked as a postdoctoral researcher in Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale from June 2007 to May 2009. He worked in Carnegie Mellon University as a visiting scholar from November 2013 to November 2014. Currently he is a Professor of Anhui University. His research fields are the synthesis, assembly, characterization and applications of nanomaterials.

Jing-Shuai Chen received his Ph.D. degree in chemical egineering from Tianjin University in 2015. He is now a lecturer in Anhui University. His research interests are synthesis and applications of nanostructured materials.

He-Lin Niu received his Ph.D. degree in University of Science and Technology of China in 2004. Then he worked in Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale and Korea University as a post doc fellow one after another. Currently, he is a Professor of Anhui University. His current research interest is in material chemistry and nanosciences.

Chang-Jie Mao received his Ph.D. degree in Nanjing University in 2008. He studied in Case Western Reserve University as a joint-supervision doctoral student from January 2007 to January 2008. Now he is working at the Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Department in Anhui University as an Associate Professor. His research interests are analytic electrochemistry and biosensor.
Sheng-Yi Zhang received his Ph.D. degree in Nanjing University in 2006. Currently he is a Professor of Anhui University. His research interests are electrochemistry and synthesis of nanomaterials.
Yu-Hua Shen graduated from AnQing Normal University in 1982. Now she is a Professor of Anhui University. Her research fields are biological mineralization and inorganic chemistry.
